text: The video shows the severely dilated gastric remnant, visibly distended within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon identifies and divides adhesions involving the BP limb, using energy to separate a thick adhesion at the proximal portion of the limb.
The surgeon divides adhesions involving the rullum, using surgical instruments to separate the fibrous tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon places a G-tube into the visibly dilated gastric remnant during the diagnostic laparoscopy.
The surgeon replaces the ports at their previous positions and performs lysis of adhesions. The three limbs of the gastric bypass are visible in the surgical field.
The video shows the third portion of the duodenum, which appears visibly distended.
The surgeon exposes the duodenum by dividing the vascular aspect of the transverse mesocolon, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs a Kocher maneuver on the duodenum, revealing the underlying inferior vena cava.
The surgical team completes the sectioning process, reaching the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) vessels, which are now fully exposed.

A pediatric endoscope is inserted through the gastrostomy and advanced into the duodenum. The endoscope navigates to the third portion of the duodenum, where an area of narrowing is observed, along with visible transmitted pulsations from the superior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon performs a duodenal jejunal anastomosis using the common channel jejunum, placing an anchoring suture to secure the connection.
The surgeon creates enterotomies and performs a stapled anastomosis between the intestinal segments.
The surgeon approximates the edges of the common enterotomy using interrupted sutures, followed by a stapled closure to secure the tissue.
The surgeon places an anti-obstruction stitch near the anastomosis site and positions a drain in the area to manage fluid accumulation.
